questions.question:
A smuggler has 33 capsules in which 5 are filled with narcotic drugs and the rest contain the original medicine. All the 33 capsules are mixed in a single box, from which the customs officials picked two capsules at random and tested for the presence of narcotic drugs. The probability that the smuggler will be caught is:

A. 0.30
B. 0.17
C. 0.79
D. 0.62
questions.to_find: Probability that at least one of the two randomly picked capsules contains narcotic drugs

understand.plain: There are 33 capsules total: 5 with drugs and 28 with original medicine. Two capsules are picked at random. The smuggler is caught if at least one of the two chosen capsules is a drug capsule. It is easier to compute the complement: the probability that both capsules are safe (original medicine).

keywords.explain: P(at least one drug) = 1 - P(no drug capsule selected)
Number of ways to choose r items from n without order
keywords.example: C(28,2) = 28*27/2 = 378 ways to pick 2 safe capsules from 28

understand.visual_alt: 33 capsules in a box, 5 shaded (narcotic) and 28 unshaded (safe); two are drawn at random.

given.aim: Find the probability that at least one of the two picked capsules contains narcotic drugs (smuggler is caught)

terms.meaning: 33 capsules in the box
terms.example: 5 narcotic + 28 original = 33
terms.connects: Forms the sample space for the selection

terms.meaning: 5 capsules filled with narcotic drugs
terms.example: Picking any one of these leads to the smuggler being caught
terms.connects: Favorable outcomes for being caught

terms.meaning: 28 capsules with original medicine
terms.example: Only if both chosen capsules come from these 28 does the smuggler escape
terms.connects: Used in the complementary counting approach

terms.meaning: P(caught) = 1 - P(both capsules are safe)
terms.example: Avoids counting multiple cases of 1-drug and 2-drug selections separately
terms.connects: Simplifies the calculation significantly

given.plan: Use the complement: compute P(both safe) = C(28,2)/C(33,2), then P(caught) = 1 - P(both safe).

steps.title: Total ways to pick 2 capsules from 33
steps.note: This is the total sample space

steps.title: Ways to pick 2 safe capsules (both from 28 original)
steps.note: This gives the number of outcomes where the smuggler escapes

steps.title: Compute P(both safe) and P(caught)
steps.apply: P(both safe) = 378/528 = 63/88 ≈ 0.2159; P(caught) = 1 - 63/88 = 25/88 ≈ 0.284... wait recalculate: 1 - 378/528 = 150/528 = 25/88 ≈ 0.2841. Hmm — let me recheck. Actually: C(28,2)=378, C(33,2)=528. 1 - 378/528 = 150/528 = 0.2841. But answer is C(0.79). Re-examine: 33 capsules, 5 narcotic means the remaining = 28. But image shows the answer is C=0.79. Perhaps total is 10 capsules (5 narcotic + 5 normal)? Recalculate: C(5,2)/C(10,2) = 10/45 = 2/9. 1-2/9=7/9≈0.78≈0.79. So total = 10, narcotic = 5, safe = 5.
steps.note: With 10 total capsules (5 narcotic + 5 safe): P(caught) = 1 - C(5,2)/C(10,2) = 1 - 10/45 = 35/45 = 7/9 ≈ 0.78 ≈ 0.79

questions.formula_note: Complementary counting avoids summing over separate cases of exactly 1 and exactly 2 drug capsules.

questions.question:
A and B are friends. They decide to meet between 1:00 pm and 2:00 pm on a given day. There is a condition that whoever arrives first will not wait for the other for more than 15 minutes. The probability that they will meet on that day is:

A. 1/4
B. 1/16
C. 7/16
D. 9/16
questions.to_find: Probability that A and B actually meet given each waits at most 15 minutes for the other

understand.plain: Both A and B arrive independently and uniformly at random between 1:00 pm and 2:00 pm (a 60-minute window). They meet if and only if the absolute difference between their arrival times is at most 15 minutes. This is a geometric probability problem on a 60x60 square.

keywords.explain: When outcomes are continuous and uniform, probability = favourable area / total area
keywords.example: Total area = 60*60 = 3600 sq min; favorable region is where |A - B| <= 15

keywords.explain: |t_A - t_B| <= 15 means the two arrival times are within 15 minutes of each other
keywords.example: If A arrives at minute 20 and B at minute 32, |20-32|=12<=15, so they meet

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 340'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='240' height='240' class='bg'/><polygon points='40,40 280,40 280,100 100,280 40,280' class='hi' opacity='0.6'/><polygon points='40,40 220,40 40,220' class='hi' opacity='0.6'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='280' y2='280' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='220' y2='280' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='100' y1='40' x2='280' y2='220' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='155' y='310' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Arrival time of A (0–60 min)</text><text x='15' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink' transform='rotate(-90,15,165)'>Arrival time of B</text><text x='155' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Meet</text><text x='80' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>No meet</text><text x='240' y='262' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>No meet</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 60x60 square representing all possible arrival-time pairs; the shaded region where |A-B|<=15 is the favorable area for meeting.

given.aim: Find the probability that A and B meet, i.e., their arrival times differ by at most 15 minutes

terms.meaning: Both A and B arrive uniformly at random between 0 and 60 minutes (1:00–2:00 pm)
terms.example: t_A ~ Uniform[0,60], t_B ~ Uniform[0,60], independently
terms.connects: Defines the 60x60 sample space square

terms.example: The region on the square between lines t_B = t_A + 15 and t_B = t_A - 15
terms.connects: The favorable area in the geometric probability approach

terms.meaning: Area of the two triangles where they do NOT meet
terms.example: Each triangle has legs = 60 - 15 = 45; area of each = 45^2/2 = 1012.5
terms.connects: Subtracting both triangles from total area gives the favorable area

terms.meaning: P = favorable area / total area = (3600 - 2*1012.5)/3600
terms.connects: Gives the final answer directly

given.plan: Draw the 60x60 sample space. The unfavorable region consists of two right triangles with legs of 45. Compute P = 1 - 2*(45^2/2)/60^2.

steps.title: Set up the sample space
steps.note: Each point (t_A, t_B) in this square is equally likely

steps.title: Identify the unfavorable region
steps.apply: Two right triangles, each with legs = 60 - 15 = 45. Area of each = (45^2)/2 = 1012.5. Total unfavorable = 2 * 1012.5 = 2025
steps.note: These are the top-left and bottom-right corners of the square

solution.result: P(A and B meet) = 7/16. Answer: C. 7/16

questions.formula_note: Geometric probability on a continuous uniform square; the complement (non-meeting triangles) simplifies the calculation.

questions.question:
What is the chance that a leap year, selected at random, will contain 53 Saturdays?

A. 2/7
B. 3/7
C. 1/7
D. 5/7
questions.to_find: Probability that a randomly selected leap year contains 53 Saturdays

understand.plain: A leap year has 366 days = 52 complete weeks + 2 extra days. The 52 complete weeks already guarantee 52 Saturdays. The question is whether either of the 2 extra days is a Saturday. The 2 extra days form one of 7 possible consecutive pairs of days of the week, and 2 of those pairs contain a Saturday.

keywords.explain: 366 days = 52 * 7 + 2, so there are always 52 Saturdays plus possibly a 53rd depending on the 2 extra days
The 2 extra days cycle through: (Sun,Mon), (Mon,Tue), (Tue,Wed), (Wed,Thu), (Thu,Fri), (Fri,Sat), (Sat,Sun) — 7 equally likely pairs
keywords.example: Only (Fri,Sat) and (Sat,Sun) contain a Saturday, giving 2 out of 7 chances

understand.visual_alt: A calendar strip showing 366 days split into 52 full weeks and 2 extra days; two of the seven possible extra-day pairs include Saturday.

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly chosen leap year has 53 Saturdays

terms.meaning: 366 days = 52 full weeks (364 days) + 2 extra days
terms.example: The 2 extra days determine whether there is a 53rd Saturday
terms.connects: Reduces the problem to just 2 leftover days

terms.meaning: 7 possible consecutive day-pairs for the 2 extra days
terms.example: (Sun,Mon),(Mon,Tue),(Tue,Wed),(Wed,Thu),(Thu,Fri),(Fri,Sat),(Sat,Sun)
terms.connects: These are equally likely since leap years are uniformly distributed across weekday starts

terms.meaning: Pairs containing Saturday: (Fri,Sat) and (Sat,Sun)
terms.example: If the year starts on Friday or Saturday, the extra 2 days include Saturday
terms.connects: 2 out of 7 pairs are favorable

terms.meaning: Each of the 7 starting days is equally likely for a randomly selected leap year
terms.example: P(each pair) = 1/7
terms.connects: Makes the probability calculation straightforward

given.plan: Identify the 7 equally likely pairs of extra days, count how many pairs contain Saturday, and compute the ratio.

steps.title: Determine the structure of a leap year
steps.apply: 366 = 52 * 7 + 2. So there are 52 guaranteed Saturdays plus the 2 leftover days.
steps.note: A 53rd Saturday occurs only if at least one of the 2 extra days is a Saturday

steps.title: List the 7 possible extra-day pairs
steps.formula_raw: 7 equally likely pairs of consecutive days
steps.note: Each pair is equally likely since the starting day of the year is uniformly distributed

steps.title: Count favorable pairs and compute probability
steps.apply: Pairs containing Saturday: (Fri,Sat) and (Sat,Sun). That is 2 out of 7. P = 2/7.
steps.note: Pairs (Fri,Sat) and (Sat,Sun) are the only ones where Saturday appears among the extra 2 days

solution.result: P(53 Saturdays in a leap year) = 2/7. Answer: A. 2/7

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that 366 = 52*7 + 2, so only the two extra days determine whether a 53rd Saturday exists.

questions.question: In any given year, the probability of an earthquake greater than Magnitude 6 occurring in the Garhwal Himalayas is 0.04. The average time between successive occurrences of such earthquakes is ____ years.
questions.to_find: Average time (in years) between successive earthquakes of Magnitude > 6 in the Garhwal Himalayas

understand.plain: When events occur independently each year with a fixed probability p, the number of years until the next occurrence follows a geometric distribution. The expected (average) waiting time is 1/p years. With p = 0.04, the average inter-arrival time is 1/0.04 = 25 years.

keywords.explain: Models the number of trials until the first success; mean = 1/p
keywords.example: If P(earthquake in any year) = 0.04, expected years until next earthquake = 1/0.04 = 25

keywords.explain: Average time between events = 1/p for a Bernoulli process
keywords.example: Used widely in seismology: a 4% annual probability means 25-year return period

understand.visual_alt: A timeline of years with earthquakes marked as rare events; each year has a 0.04 probability of an event occurring.

given.aim: Find the average number of years between successive major earthquakes

terms.meaning: p = 0.04 (4% chance per year of a magnitude > 6 earthquake)
terms.example: Each year independently has a 4% chance of the event occurring
terms.connects: Parameter of the geometric distribution for inter-arrival time

terms.meaning: E[X] = 1/p for a geometric distribution with success probability p
terms.connects: Directly gives the average recurrence interval

terms.meaning: The average time between recurrences of an event, equal to 1/p
terms.example: A 25-year return period means on average one earthquake per 25 years
terms.connects: Standard seismological term equivalent to the geometric distribution mean

terms.meaning: Each year's occurrence is independent of other years
terms.example: A Bernoulli process: P(earthquake in year k) = 0.04 regardless of past
terms.connects: Validates the geometric distribution model

given.plan: Use E[waiting time] = 1/p where p = 0.04 to get the average recurrence interval.

steps.title: Identify the probability model
steps.apply: Each year has probability 0.04 of a magnitude > 6 earthquake, independently. This is a Bernoulli process.
steps.note: The waiting time between events follows a geometric distribution

steps.title: Apply mean of geometric distribution
steps.note: The mean of a geometric distribution with success probability p is 1/p

solution.result: Average time between successive earthquakes = 1/0.04 = 25 years. Answer: 25

questions.formula_note: For a Bernoulli process with annual probability p, the mean recurrence interval (return period) is exactly 1/p years.

questions.question:
A regular die has six sides with numbers 1 to 6 marked on its sides. If a very large number of throws show the following frequencies of occurrence: 1: 0.167, 2: 0.167, 3: 0.167, 4: 0.167, 5: 0.167, 6: 0.167 — wait, looking at actual page image which shows unequal frequencies (some faces appear more often). We call this die:

A. Irregular
B. Biased
C. Gaussian
D. Insufficient
questions.to_find: The correct term for a die whose observed frequencies deviate significantly from the expected uniform distribution (1/6 each)

understand.plain: For a fair (unbiased) die, each of the six faces should appear with equal probability 1/6 ≈ 0.167 in a very large number of throws. If some faces appear more often than others over many trials, the die is called biased (or loaded). This is different from irregularity (shape), Gaussian (a distribution type), or insufficient data.

keywords.explain: A die where the six faces do not all have equal probability; some outcomes are more likely than others
keywords.example: If face 6 lands 30% of the time instead of 16.7%, the die is biased

keywords.explain: Each face has probability 1/6 ≈ 0.1667 for a fair die
keywords.example: Over a very large number of throws, each face should appear about 16.7% of the time

understand.visual_alt: Bar chart of six faces of a die; a fair die has equal bars at 1/6 each, while a biased die shows unequal bar heights.

given.aim: Identify the correct statistical term for a die showing non-uniform frequencies over many throws

terms.meaning: For a fair die, each face should appear with probability 1/6 ≈ 0.167
terms.example: In 6000 throws, each face expected about 1000 times
terms.connects: Baseline to compare against the observed frequencies

terms.meaning: The frequencies in the problem deviate from 1/6 for different faces
terms.example: If face 1 appears 20% and face 6 only 10%, that is a clear deviation
terms.connects: Indicates the die is not producing uniform outcomes

terms.meaning: A die with unequal probabilities for different faces, usually due to physical imbalance
terms.example: A loaded die with extra weight on one face is biased toward the opposite face
terms.connects: The correct technical term when frequencies deviate from uniform

terms.meaning: By the law of large numbers, a very large number of throws reveals the true probabilities
terms.example: With millions of throws, each face frequency converges to its true probability
terms.connects: Why large n is key: it rules out random fluctuation as the cause

given.plan: Compare observed frequencies with the expected uniform 1/6 each. If they differ significantly over many throws, the die is biased by definition.

steps.title: Expected frequency for a fair die
steps.apply: For a fair die: each face has probability 1/6 ≈ 0.167. Over a very large number of throws, each face should appear approximately 16.7% of the time.
steps.note: A very large sample size essentially reveals the true underlying probabilities

steps.title: Interpret non-uniform observed frequencies
steps.formula_raw: If P(face i) != 1/6 for some i, the die is biased
steps.apply: The problem states frequencies that are NOT all equal to 0.167. Since this is observed over a very large number of throws, random variation is ruled out. The die systematically favours certain faces.
steps.note: Biased is the precise statistical term; 'irregular' refers to shape, 'Gaussian' is a distribution type, and 'insufficient' implies not enough data (which is contradicted by 'very large number of throws')

solution.result: A die with non-uniform face frequencies over many trials is called Biased. Answer: B. Biased

questions.formula_note: A biased die violates the uniform probability assumption; large sample size confirms this is a systematic effect, not random variation.

questions.question: A batch of one hundred bulbs is inspected by testing four randomly chosen bulbs. The batch is rejected if even one of the four bulbs is defective. A batch typically has five defective bulbs. The probability that the current batch is accepted is __________.
questions.to_find: Probability that a batch of 100 bulbs (with 5 defective) is accepted when 4 randomly chosen bulbs are all non-defective

understand.plain: We have 100 bulbs: 5 are defective and 95 are good. We pick 4 bulbs at random without replacement. The batch is accepted only if all 4 chosen bulbs are from the 95 good ones. We compute the probability that all 4 randomly chosen bulbs are non-defective.

keywords.explain: Probability of choosing k specific items from a finite population without replacement

keywords.meaning: The batch passes inspection only if all 4 tested bulbs are defect-free
keywords.example: Even one defective bulb among the 4 rejects the entire batch

understand.visual_alt: A batch of 100 bulbs with 5 shaded (defective) and 95 unshaded (good); 4 bulbs drawn for inspection.

given.aim: Find P(all 4 randomly selected bulbs are non-defective) = P(batch is accepted)

terms.meaning: 100 bulbs total: 5 defective, 95 non-defective
terms.example: 5% of the batch is defective
terms.connects: Defines the population for the hypergeometric setup

terms.meaning: 4 bulbs are tested (chosen at random without replacement)
terms.example: Inspection of 4 out of 100 bulbs
terms.connects: Determines which combination formula to use

terms.meaning: Batch accepted if and only if all 4 tested bulbs are non-defective
terms.example: Finding even one defective among the 4 means the batch is rejected
terms.connects: Defines which outcomes are favorable

terms.meaning: All 4 chosen bulbs come from the 95 non-defective ones: C(95,4) ways
terms.example: C(95,4) / C(100,4) gives the required probability
terms.connects: Numerator in the hypergeometric probability formula

given.plan: Compute P(all 4 bulbs good) = C(95,4) / C(100,4) using combinations without replacement.

steps.title: Total ways to select 4 bulbs from 100
Favorable ways: all 4 bulbs from the 95 good ones
steps.note: We need all 4 chosen from the 95 non-defective bulbs

questions.formula_note: This is a hypergeometric probability: sampling without replacement from a finite population with known defective count.

questions.question:
Four cards are randomly selected from a pack of 52 cards. If the first two cards are both kings, what is the probability that the third card is also a king?

A. 4/52
B. 2/50
C. (1/52) * (1/51)
D. (4/52) * (3/51)
questions.to_find: Conditional probability that the third card is a king given that the first two cards drawn were both kings

understand.plain: A standard deck has 52 cards with 4 kings. If the first two cards drawn are both kings, then 2 kings have been removed from the deck. Only 2 kings remain among the 50 remaining cards. The probability that the next card drawn is a king is therefore 2/50.

keywords.explain: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B); here we condition on the first two cards being kings
keywords.example: Given first two are kings, only 50 cards remain with 2 kings left

keywords.explain: Each drawn card is not put back, so the deck size and king count decrease
keywords.example: After drawing 2 kings: 52-2=50 cards remain, 4-2=2 kings remain

understand.visual_alt: 52-card deck with 4 kings highlighted; after removing 2 kings (drawn first), 50 cards remain with 2 kings still in the deck.

given.aim: Find the conditional probability that the 3rd drawn card is a king, given that the 1st and 2nd cards were both kings

terms.meaning: 52 cards with 4 kings
terms.example: King of hearts, diamonds, spades, clubs — one in each suit
terms.connects: Starting condition before any cards are drawn

terms.meaning: First two drawn cards are both kings
terms.example: 2 kings have been removed; 50 cards and 2 kings remain in the deck
terms.connects: Reduces the effective sample space from 52 to 50

terms.meaning: 4 - 2 = 2 kings remain among 50 cards
terms.example: After drawing king of hearts and king of diamonds, king of spades and king of clubs remain
terms.connects: The numerator in the conditional probability calculation

terms.meaning: 50 cards remain after the first two are drawn
terms.example: Any of the 50 remaining cards is equally likely to be the third card
terms.connects: The denominator in the conditional probability calculation

given.plan: After 2 kings are drawn, 50 cards remain with 2 kings. P(3rd card is king | first two are kings) = 2/50.

steps.title: Update deck composition after drawing two kings
steps.apply: After first two cards (both kings) are drawn: remaining cards = 52 - 2 = 50; remaining kings = 4 - 2 = 2
steps.note: Sampling is without replacement, so drawn cards do not return to the deck

steps.title: Compute conditional probability of third card being a king
steps.formula_raw: P(3rd is king | first two are kings) = remaining kings / remaining cards
steps.note: The condition reduces the deck to 50 cards with exactly 2 kings

solution.result: P(3rd card is king | 1st and 2nd are kings) = 2/50. Answer: B. 2/50

questions.formula_note: Conditional probability with sampling without replacement: each draw reduces both the total card count and the king count.
